items.rationale: He views sum-check as the right primitive to attack the real scalability bottleneck (prover time) because it minimizes the prover's cryptographic commitment costs, and believes good hardware implementations will follow.
items.age_note: First stated 2022-12-28 and repeatedly, more forcefully, reaffirmed through 2023-08; this is his current and strengthening stance, not stale.

citations.quote: sum-check-based SNARKs remain the most promising for minimizing total prover work
the scalability bottleneck for awhile now has been prover time. And the right technique to unlock this is sum-check

items.rationale: Lasso is a new lookup argument with a far faster prover (benchmarked ~10x over Halo2, expecting 30-40x optimized) that commits only to small field elements, enabling simpler, more auditable SNARKs.

citations.quote: Lasso is a new lookup argument with a much faster prover
the initial Lasso implementation is about 10x faster than Halo2's lookup argument

items.rationale: Jolt is his proposed new paradigm for zkVMs built on Lasso, decomposing instructions into lookups to slash prover commitment costs versus hand-coded optimized circuits.

citations.quote: Jolt builds on Lasso to offer a new paradigm in zkVM design
why are we spending millions of dollars and thousands of person-hours hand-coding optimized circuits, rather than just specifying evaluation tables?

items.rationale: He expects hashing-based, field-agnostic commitments like Brakedown to beat elliptic-curve commitments whenever working over small fields makes sense, especially as recursion struggles get solved.
items.age_note: Most recent stance (2023-08-31/2023-09-03); nuances his earlier July skepticism that small fields are uniformly powerful — he now sees a clear role for them.

citations.quote: I'm excited about this approach to polynomial commitments and hopeful we'll see further improvements soon.
for Brakedown to be better than EC-based commitments whenever working over small fields makes sense

items.rationale: He believes blockchains will reshape how society establishes trust and privacy and that the SNARK space is in an explosion of innovation with transformative potential.

citations.quote: I believe blockchains will transform society's mechanisms for establishing trust & privacy
This space is experiencing an explosion of innovation, and I think this technology can be transformative

items.rationale: He expects super-efficient, circuit-avoiding protocols to unlock applications like neural-network inference/training and proving statements about crypto-systems that inherently need large fields.
items.age_note: Stated 2022-12-31, reinforced 2023-07 in the large-field discussion; not restated as a focus since, but consistent with current views.

citations.quote: neural network training/inference, the bottleneck in which is often repeated matrix multiplication of small integers
many applications involve proving statements about crypto-systems that inherently involve large fields

items.rationale: He warns that known attacks on FRI at 80 bits run non-interactively are feasible and pose systemic risk to the rollup ecosystem when securing hundreds of millions in assets.
items.age_note: Sustained campaign from 2022-09 through 2023-04; the dYdX StarkEx 80-bit concern was reaffirmed as still unresolved on 2023-04-18 — his most recent word on it.

citations.quote: Known attacks on FRI when run non-interactively at 80 bits of security are feasible.
The dYdX StarkEx verifier remains at 80 bits of security today.

items.rationale: He fears the community's tension between performance and security gets resolved toward performance, risking lost trust and misappropriated funds.

citations.quote: if we prioritize performance over security, we may cause people to lose trust in the technology
some deployed SNARKs are being run at "80 bits of security" - which is not as well-defined a term as it may seem

items.rationale: He remains cautious that all implemented composed SNARKs rely on heuristic security or unquantified concrete-security loss, which grows with composition depth.
items.age_note: Stated late 2022; not restated in 2023 but never reversed — he calls recursion 'extremely powerful' while keeping the security caveat. Treat as persisted.

citations.quote: I remain cautious about security especially at large depth
All implemented composed SNARKs have heuristic security or at least significant loss in concrete security can't be ruled out

items.rationale: He cautions that projects deploy Poseidon at low security levels, so relaxing on hash-function security is premature.
items.age_note: Stated 2022-12-31 and not restated through 2023-09; cite with age — still his standing view absent any reversal.

citations.quote: while projects are deploying Poseidon with at most 95 bits of security, it's premature to relax on this.

items.rationale: He argues large-field FRI deployments incur severe prover slowdowns (citing a ~33x figure) and that benchmarks are often cherry-picked to favor FRI's best-case parameters.

citations.quote: using the 252-bit field is resulting in a 33x slowdown for the prover
Polygon zkEVM requires 250+ GBs of space

items.rationale: He sees public debate as deeply confused, conflating what projects actually do versus what they could do, and lacking rigorous cost accounting — which he tries to correct.

citations.quote: the public discussion in this space has been deeply confused
I identify, and try to clear up, a variety of misconceptions that have been hindering progress and causing confusion

risk_regime.rationale: Thaler is a cryptography researcher; his entire corpus concerns SNARK design, security, and performance, with zero statements on token prices, allocation, or market timing. He shows strong STRUCTURAL technological optimism about ZK/blockchain ('this technology can be transformative') paired with acute caution on security of live deployments — but expresses no market-level risk-on/risk-off posture, so inferring one would be extrapolation beyond his words.

notes: Justin Thaler's 'market view' is a technology-thesis view, not an asset view. Dominant and rising signal (Aug-Sep 2023): a strong bet that sum-check-based proving + the lookup-singularity (Lasso/Jolt) is the path to fast provers, set explicitly against FRI/large-field and proof-size-optimized SNARKs (PlonK, Marlin, Groth16, Halo2). His longest-running and highest-conviction theme is a security watchdog stance on under-secured deployments — repeatedly naming dYdX's StarkEx 80-bit FRI verifier as too low (2022-09 through 2023-04). Projects he names (StarkWare/StarkNet, dYdX StarkEx, Polygon zkEVM, RISC Zero, Matter Labs, Aztec) are discussed as technical artifacts, not investments, so they are NOT listed as tokens. Recency weighting: small-fields stance evolved from July 2023 skepticism ('counterpoint to small fields being powerful') to a balanced Sep 2023 view that small fields and Brakedown-style commitments both 'have a role to play' — current stance is the nuanced/balanced one. Recursion and Poseidon concerns are older (late 2022) and not restated in 2023, but never reversed, hence persisted.
